author: Augustine of Hippo
book: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
It is God’s Grace Which Specially Distinguishes One Man from Another.
title: It is God’s Grace Which Specially Distinguishes One Man from Another.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
verses.text: In this the apostle’s most evident intention, in which he speaks against human pride, so that none should glory in man but in God, it is too absurd, as I think, to suppose God’s natural gifts, whether man’s entire and perfected nature itself as it was bestowed on him in his first state, or the remains, whatever they may be, of his degraded nature.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
verses.text: For is it by such gifts as these, which are common to all men, that men are distinguished from men?

verses.book: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
verses.text: But here he first said, “For who maketh thee to differ?” and then added, “And what hast thou that thou hast not received?”

verses.book: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
verses.text: Because a man, puffed up against another, might say, “My faith makes me to differ,” or “My righteousness,” or anything else of the kind.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
verses.text: In reply to such notions, the good teacher says, “But what hast thou that thou hast not received?”

verses.book: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
verses.text: And from whom but from Him who maketh thee to differ from another, on whom He bestowed not what He bestowed on thee?

verses.book: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
verses.text: “Now if,” says he, “thou hast received it, why dost thou glory as if thou receivedst it not?”

verses.book: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
verses.text: Is he concerned, I ask, about anything else save that he who glorieth should glory in the Lord?

verses.book: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
verses.text: But nothing is so opposed to this feeling as for any one to glory concerning his own merits in such a way as if he himself had made them for himself, and not the grace of God,—a grace, however, which makes the good to differ from the wicked, and is not common to the good and the wicked.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
verses.text: Let the grace, therefore, whereby we are living and reasonable creatures, and are distinguished from cattle, be attributed to nature; let that grace also by which, among men themselves, the handsome are made to differ from the ill-formed, or the intelligent from the stupid, or anything of that kind, be ascribed to nature.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
verses.text: But he whom the apostle was rebuking did not puff himself up as contrasted with cattle, nor as contrasted with any other man, in respect of any natural endowment which might be found even in the worst of men.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
verses.text: But he ascribed to himself, and not to God, some good gift which pertained to a holy life, and was puffed up therewith when he deserved to hear the rebuke, “Who hath made thee to differ? and what hast thou that thou receivedst not?”

verses.book: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
verses.text: For though the capacity to have faith is of nature, is it also of nature to have it?

verses.book: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
verses.text: “For all men have not faith,” although all men have the capacity to have faith.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
verses.text: But the apostle does not say, “And what hast thou capacity to have, the capacity to have which thou receivedst not?” but he says, “And what hast thou which thou receivedst not?”

verses.book: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
verses.text: Accordingly, the capacity to have faith, as the capacity to have love, belongs to men’s nature; but to have faith, even as to have love, belongs to the grace of believers.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
verses.text: That nature, therefore, in which is given to us the capacity of having faith, does not distinguish man from man, but faith itself makes the believer to differ from the unbeliever.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
verses.text: And thus, when it is said, “For who maketh thee to differ? and what hast thou that thou receivedst not?” if any one dare to say, “I have faith of myself, I did not, therefore, receive it,” he directly contradicts this most manifest truth,—not because it is not in the choice of man’s will to believe or not to believe, but because in the elect the will is prepared by the Lord.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
verses.text: Thus, moreover, the passage, “For who maketh thee to differ? and what hast thou that thou receivedst not?” refers to that very faith which is in the will of man.
